topics.title: Amazon RDS & Aurora — Managed Databases, Multi-AZ & Read Replicas
topics.description: Amazon RDS eliminates undifferentiated DB management work — hardware, OS patches, automated backups, and Multi-AZ failover are handled by AWS. Multi-AZ creates a synchronous standby in another AZ for high availability (automatic DNS failover in 60–120 seconds, zero data loss). Read Replicas are asynchronous copies for read scaling (up to 5 for RDS MySQL, 15 for Aurora). Amazon Aurora is AWS's cloud-native RDBMS: 5× MySQL throughput, 6 copies of data across 3 AZs in a distributed storage cluster, failover in under 30 seconds. RDS Proxy manages connection pooling as a managed service — critical when Lambda functions create thousands of short-lived connections that would otherwise exhaust the DB connection limit.
topics.story: Running MySQL on EC2 is like owning your own bakery building — you handle plumbing leaks, roof repairs, and fire drills yourself. RDS is renting a managed commercial kitchen: AWS handles the building (hardware, OS, patches, backups). If the kitchen catches fire (AZ failure), your lease automatically moves to the backup kitchen in another building (Multi-AZ failover, 60-120 seconds). Aurora is franchising a high-tech kitchen with six ovens spread across three buildings — all ovens share the same dough (distributed storage). Even if one building floods, five ovens keep baking without you noticing a single missed loaf.
topics.analogy: Multi-AZ = co-pilot who mirrors every action synchronously (zero lag, zero data loss, takes over in 60s if primary fails — HA not performance). Read Replica = photocopier running async (slight delay acceptable, scales read traffic — performance not HA). Aurora storage = 6-way replicated across 3 AZs automatically, no config needed. RDS Proxy = managed connection pool (prevents Lambda connection storms). Parameter Group = DB configuration file (innodb_buffer_pool_size, max_connections — create new, attach, reboot required). Automated Backup = daily snapshot + transaction logs = restore to any second within retention window (1–35 days).
topics.memoryAnchor: Multi-AZ: HA not performance (synchronous, 1 standby, same region, auto-failover). Read Replica: performance not HA (asynchronous, up to 5/15, cross-region possible, replication lag). Aurora: distributed storage 6 copies 3 AZs, fastest failover < 30s, up to 15 replicas. RDS Proxy: connection pooling managed service (must for Lambda). Parameter Group: DB config (reboot required for static params). Snapshot: manual backup retained until deleted. Automated Backup: daily + binlogs PITR within retention.
topics.firstPrinciples: Multi-AZ synchronous replication = zero data loss but only one standby and only HA (not read scaling). Read Replicas async = replication lag possible — reads may be slightly stale. Aurora solves both differently: the storage layer replicates 6 copies independently of compute. Failover is just pointing a new compute instance at the same shared storage — no data transfer needed, which is why Aurora failover is 10× faster than RDS Multi-AZ. Choose Multi-AZ for availability, Read Replicas for throughput.
topics.bruteForce: Single EC2 MySQL instance with manual cron backups. When the EC2 fails at 3 AM, restore from backup and lose hours of data. Manually promote a read replica to primary on failure (20+ minute MTTR with human in the loop). Hard-code the primary endpoint — when failover happens, app still points to dead host until someone updates config.
topics.optimizedApproach: RDS Multi-AZ for production OLTP (automatic DNS failover, no code change in app). Read Replicas for analytics and reporting queries (direct these to the replica endpoint). RDS Proxy for Lambda/serverless to pool connections. Aurora for > 5K write IOPS or when failover speed matters. Point application to the cluster endpoint (not instance endpoint) — Aurora routes to current primary automatically.
topics.whenToUse: RDS Multi-AZ: any production database where downtime is costly. Read Replicas: reporting, analytics, > 80% read traffic. Aurora: > 5K write IOPS, need < 30s failover, up to 15 read replicas, global multi-region. RDS Proxy: Lambda or containers accessing RDS (prevents connection exhaustion). Aurora Serverless v2: dev/test, bursty workloads (auto-scales from 0.5 ACU, pauses when idle).

examples.title: Spring Boot Read Replica Routing — AbstractRoutingDataSource + AOP
examples.explanation: Aurora exposes two endpoints: the cluster endpoint (always routes to current primary, for writes) and the reader endpoint (round-robins across all read replicas). AbstractRoutingDataSource picks a DataSource per-thread. The AOP aspect intercepts @Transactional(readOnly=true) and switches the thread-local to REPLICA before the method runs, then clears it in finally. Zero changes to service or repository code — routing is transparent. HikariCP replica pool is sized larger (40 vs 20) because replica handles more concurrent reads than the write primary.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: topic listing page is read-heavy — route to replica. Submission save routes to primary (must be consistent). Replica lag in Aurora is typically < 20ms (shared storage, only redo log shipped). For read-after-write (profile page right after update), use primary to avoid stale replica data.

examples.title: RDS Parameter Group Tuning & Key CloudWatch Alerts
examples.explanation: Parameter group changes: dynamic parameters (like slow_query_log) apply immediately. Static parameters (like innodb_buffer_pool_size) require a reboot. You cannot edit the default parameter group — create a new one, modify it, attach to the DB instance, then reboot. BufferCacheHitRatio is the single most important RDS health metric: below 0.95 means 5%+ of data access requires disk I/O. Aurora's BufferCacheHitRatio is usually > 0.99 because the storage layer keeps hot data in the distributed buffer pool across all instances.
examples.realWorldUse: A common production mistake: deploy on a db.t3.medium with default parameter group (128MB buffer pool). Under load, BufferCacheHitRatio drops to 0.70 and CPU spikes — the DB is doing disk reads for 30% of queries. Fix: new parameter group + innodb_buffer_pool_size = 3GB, reboot during maintenance window. DevLearner: if submissions table grows to 50M rows, buffer pool must hold the hot indexes in memory to keep query latency under 5ms.

problems.title: Read Replica Traffic Router
problems.description: Implement a ReadWriteRouter that routes DB operations to the correct endpoint. All writes and strong-consistency reads go to primary. Read-only queries go to replicas using round-robin. If the primary fails, automatically promote the first replica as the new primary.
problems.hint1: Store primary and replicas as separate fields. Route based on a boolean readOnly flag.
problems.hint2: Use AtomicInteger for round-robin: idx = counter.getAndIncrement() % replicas.size().
problems.hint3: Failover: set primary.healthy=false, promote replicas.remove(0) as new primary.

problems.title: Bounded Database Connection Pool
problems.description: Implement a thread-safe BoundedConnectionPool with a configurable max size. acquire(timeoutMs) blocks until a connection is available or the timeout expires — throwing RuntimeException on timeout. release(conn) returns the connection. Track how many times the pool timed out.
problems.hint1: Use ArrayBlockingQueue<String> pre-filled with connection strings. poll(timeout, unit) returns null on timeout.
problems.hint2: On null from poll(), increment an AtomicInteger timeouts counter, then throw RuntimeException.
problems.hint3: release() just calls pool.offer(conn). available() returns pool.size().
problems.testCases:
Available: 3
After 3 acquired: 0
After release: 1
Timeouts: 1

problems.title: Multi-AZ Failover Simulation
problems.description: Simulate a Multi-AZ RDS setup. A MultiAzClient wraps a primary and standby node. On primary failure (exception thrown), automatically fail over to standby — logging the failover event — and retry the same query. All subsequent queries use the promoted standby. If both are down, re-throw the exception.
problems.hint1: Wrap active.execute(sql) in try-catch. On exception from primary, print failover message.
problems.hint2: Set active = standby and call active.execute(sql) for the retry. Only fail over if active == primary.
problems.hint3: Track failover with a boolean so double-failure doesn't cause infinite recursion.
problems.testCases:
[db-primary.us-east-1a.rds.aws.com] SELECT 1 OK
Failover to: db-standby.us-east-1b.rds.aws.com
[db-standby.us-east-1b.rds.aws.com] SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 1 OK
[db-standby.us-east-1b.rds.aws.com] INSERT INTO events VALUES(?) OK
